fields.Identifying_Data_Chief_Complaint: 12-year-old female brought by father for persistent sadness after grandmother's death.
fields.Subjective: Patient is a 12-year-old female presenting with ongoing sadness three months after maternal grandmother's death from cancer. She reports thinking about grandmother daily and crying several times per week, particularly at bedtime. She denies any thoughts of self-harm or suicidal ideation. Patient states grandmother was her primary caregiver after school and they were very close. She feels guilty she could not visit more during grandmother's final hospitalization. Sleep is somewhat disrupted and appetite decreased but weight stable. Continues to attend school and participate in choir though with less enthusiasm. Father reports patient is processing normal grief but wants to ensure she has appropriate support. Patient has visited grandmother's grave and finds some comfort there. She has memory box of grandmother's items she looks through regularly. (Social Worker Veronica Lewis, LCSW)
fields.Psychosocial_Context: Lives with father and stepmother; maternal grandmother died three months ago after close relationship; experiencing normal grief process; some guilt about not visiting enough; maintaining basic functioning; engaged in memorial activities.
Assessment_and_Plan: Normal grief reaction following death of close family member; timeline and symptoms appropriate.
Protective factors: supportive father, engaged in healthy memorial activities, maintains function, processing grief appropriately, no suicidal ideation.
Referral for grief counseling to provide support through mourning process.
Address guilt feelings regarding visitation during illness.
Validation that grief is appropriate and will lessen with time.
Encourage continued memorial activities as helpful for processing.
Father supported in allowing patient to grieve at own pace.
Monitor for complicated grief; expect gradual improvement over 6-12 months.
Follow-up in 6 weeks; intervention if grief becomes prolonged or intensifies.
fields.Author: Veronica Lewis, LCSW (Social Worker)
